threats.title: TimelockTokenPool Withdrawal Signature Lacks Domain Separation - Cross-Chain Replay
threats.hypothesis_description: The withdraw(address,bytes) function recovers the recipient from a signature over keccak256(abi.encodePacked('Withdraw unlocked Taiko token to: ', _to)). This hash includes no chain ID, contract address, nonce, or expiry. A valid signature observed on-chain on one chain can be replayed on any other chain where the same recipient has a grant in another TimelockTokenPool instance.
threats.investigation_rationale: The signed message is deterministic and context-free. If TimelockTokenPool is deployed on multiple chains (L1, L2, testnet) with the same recipient, a signature from one chain is valid on all others. Since transactions are publicly observable after inclusion, an attacker extracts _sig from a completed tx and replays it.

proof_of_signal.pattern: keccak256(abi.encodePacked('Withdraw unlocked Taiko token to: ', _to)) has no chainid, no address(this), no nonce

preconditions: Same recipient address has grants in TimelockTokenPool on multiple chains
Recipient has executed withdraw(address,bytes) on at least one chain (signature observable on-chain)
Unlocked tokens available on target chain

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: bytes32 hash = keccak256(abi.encodePacked('Withdraw unlocked Taiko token to: ', _to))
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Signature extracted from completed transaction on chain A, replayed on chain B
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker forces recipient's tokens on chain B to be sent to _to address, which may be an address attacker controls on chain B but recipient controls on chain A

economic_questions: Are TimelockTokenPool instances deployed on multiple chains with overlapping recipients?
Can _to be a contract address controlled by attacker on the target chain?
What is the total value of grants exposed to cross-chain replay?
validation_requirements: Verify multi-chain deployment from deployment scripts
Construct cross-chain replay scenario with concrete _to address
Calculate maximum extractable value across all chains

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to entire unlocked grant amount on target chain

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after observing legitimate withdraw tx on source chain
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact, direct token theft

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to 100% of unlocked tokens on target chain

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if multiple TimelockTokenPool instances exist across chains
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify ECDSA.recover produces same address cross-chain with same signature

threats.title: PEMCertChainLib notBefore Date Tag Validation Logic Error Accepts Invalid Certificate Dates
threats.hypothesis_description: In PEMCertChainLib.decodeCert, the condition `(notBeforeTag != 0x17 && notBeforeTag == 0x18)` simplifies to `(notBeforeTag == 0x18)`. This REJECTS valid GeneralizedTime (0x18) certificates and ACCEPTS certificates with arbitrary invalid notBefore tag types (e.g., 0x05, 0x30). The intended check should be `(notBeforeTag != 0x17 && notBeforeTag != 0x18)`.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect. Valid certificates using GeneralizedTime for notBefore (dates after 2049) are rejected, causing DoS. Invalid tag types pass through to X509DateUtils.toTimestamp which may produce garbage timestamps or revert. This undermines the integrity of the certificate chain validation used for SGX attestation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: (notBeforeTag != 0x17 && notBeforeTag == 0x18) simplifies to (notBeforeTag == 0x18) — inverted from intended validation

preconditions: Certificate with GeneralizedTime (0x18) notBefore tag submitted for verification
Or certificate with non-standard tag type for notBefore

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Valid 0x18-tagged certs rejected (DoS); invalid-tagged certs may produce incorrect timestamps enabling bypass of time-based validity checks
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: If an invalid notBefore tag produces a timestamp of 0, the cert appears valid from epoch start, potentially bypassing notBefore checks in _verifyCertChain

economic_questions: Can a certificate with invalid notBefore tag pass signature verification in _verifyCertChain?
What timestamp does X509DateUtils.toTimestamp produce for non-ASCII-digit bytes?
Does this create a future DoS when Intel transitions to GeneralizedTime?
validation_requirements: Test decodeCert with notBeforeTag values 0x17, 0x18, 0x05, 0x30
Verify if garbage timestamp from invalid tag causes revert or silent acceptance
Assess impact on SGX prover registration pipeline

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: SGX attestation bypass could enable fraudulent proofs
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Protocol-level impact if attestation is bypassed

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When Intel issues certs with GeneralizedTime notBefore (future) or immediately with crafted certs
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could block SGX prover registration or enable invalid attestations

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All SGX provers and protocol users relying on attestation
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete loss of SGX proving capability (DoS scenario)

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Current Intel PCK cert date tag formats
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace X509DateUtils.toTimestamp with non-standard input bytes

threats.title: TimelockTokenPool costToWithdraw Precision Loss via Division Before Multiplication
threats.hypothesis_description: In getMyGrantSummary, costToWithdraw is computed as `(amountUnlocked / 1e18) * costPerToken - costPaid`. The integer division `amountUnlocked / 1e18` truncates up to 999999999999999999 wei (≈1 token) before multiplication. Each withdrawal cycle, the recipient pays slightly less cost than intended, effectively receiving a discount on their token purchase.
threats.investigation_rationale: The division before multiplication is a classic precision loss pattern. While each individual loss is small (up to costPerToken per withdrawal), recipients calling withdraw multiple times as tokens unlock accumulate the benefit. The code explicitly notes precision is maintained at token level not wei level.
preconditions: Grant has non-zero costPerToken
Recipient calls withdraw multiple times as tokens gradually unlock

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: amountUnlocked / 1e18 truncates before multiplying by costPerToken
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Each withdrawal truncates up to 1e18-1 wei worth of unlocked amount, reducing cost by up to costPerToken per call
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Over N withdrawal calls, recipient saves up to N * costPerToken in cost tokens

economic_questions: What is the typical costPerToken value for grants?
How many withdrawal calls could a recipient reasonably make?
Does the savings justify gas costs of multiple withdrawals?
validation_requirements: Calculate max precision loss per withdrawal
Model multi-withdrawal scenario vs single withdrawal
Compare cost savings against gas expenditure

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to N * costPerToken in cost token savings
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on costPerToken magnitude and number of withdrawals

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Minimal individual impact, accumulated over time

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol/sharedVault receiving less cost tokens than expected
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to costPerToken per withdrawal cycle

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Typical costPerToken values and grant sizes
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare expected vs actual costPaid over multi-withdrawal scenario

threats.title: ERC20Airdrop2 withdraw() Allows Anyone to Force Token Delivery to Any User
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC20Airdrop2.withdraw(address user) is externally callable by anyone with no access control or nonReentrant guard. Any third party can trigger token withdrawal for any user at any time during the withdrawal window, forcing the user to receive tokens at a time they did not choose.
threats.investigation_rationale: While tokens flow to the correct user (not stolen), the lack of access control enables economic griefing. In jurisdictions where token receipt triggers taxable events, an attacker could force users to receive tokens at unfavorable times. Additionally, without nonReentrant, if the token has callback mechanisms, reentrancy could be attempted (though state ordering mitigates double-spend).

proof_of_signal.pattern: function withdraw(address user) external ongoingWithdrawals — no msg.sender check, no nonReentrant

preconditions: Withdrawal window is active (claimEnd < block.timestamp < claimEnd + withdrawalWindow)
Target user has claimed tokens (claimedAmount[user] > 0)
Target user has non-zero withdrawable balance

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No msg.sender == user check in withdraw()
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls withdraw(victim) to force token receipt at attacker-chosen time
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack with tax implications for victims; potential reentrancy if token has callbacks

economic_questions: What are the tax/legal implications of forced token receipt?
Could this be combined with a token callback for reentrancy?
Is there any economic benefit to the attacker beyond griefing?
validation_requirements: Confirm no access control on withdraw
Test reentrancy scenario with ERC777-style tokens
Assess real-world impact of forced withdrawal timing

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During withdrawal window, at worst time for victim
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could trigger sell pressure if many users forced to receive simultaneously

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Any user who has claimed in ERC20Airdrop2
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Forced receipt of linearly unlocked tokens at attacker-chosen time
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All claimed but not-yet-withdrawn airdrop amounts

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if airdrop token supports callbacks
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify reentrancy safety with state-before-interaction pattern

threats.title: PEMCertChainLib _findTcb Array Out-of-Bounds Revert on Non-Standard SVN Ordering
threats.hypothesis_description: In _findTcb, cpusvns is allocated with size 16 (SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE) but the loop iterates 17 times (SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE + 1). If the PCESVN OID appears at any position other than the last (i=16), the final iteration writes to cpusvns[16] which is out of bounds, causing a Solidity 0.8.x revert. Additionally, when PCESVN appears mid-sequence, cpusvns retains a 0 at the PCESVN's index, corrupting TCB level matching.
threats.investigation_rationale: While standard Intel PCK certificates have PCESVN as the last SVN entry, this assumption is not validated. If Intel changes their certificate format or a different CA issues certs with different ordering, all attestation verification permanently reverts. The cpusvns gap (zero at PCESVN's index) also causes incorrect TCB matching even without OOB.

proof_of_signal.pattern: cpusvns = new uint256[](SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE) with loop iterating SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE+1 times; else branch writes cpusvns[i] where i can be 16

preconditions: PCK certificate has PCESVN OID at a position other than the last SVN entry
Quote containing such certificate is submitted for verification

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: cpusvns[i] where i ranges 0..16 but array size is 16 (indices 0..15)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Cert with non-standard SVN ordering causes permanent DoS of attestation verification
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Blocks all SGX prover registration, enabling alternative attack vectors on proof system

economic_questions: Does Intel guarantee PCESVN is always the last SVN component?
What happens to the protocol if SGX attestation is permanently DoS'd?
Can an attacker craft a cert that triggers OOB but still passes earlier checks?
validation_requirements: Test _findTcb with PCESVN at various positions
Verify Intel PCK certificate SVN ordering specification
Assess impact on SGX proving tier if attestation is blocked

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: blocking SGX provers could affect proof economics

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: If Intel changes SVN ordering in PCK certs
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could disable SGX proving tier entirely

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete inability to register SGX instances

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Intel PCK certificate specification for SVN ordering

threats.title: Root CA Certificate Expiry and Self-Signature Not Verified in _verifyCertChain
threats.hypothesis_description: In _verifyCertChain, when processing a 3-cert chain [PCK, Intermediate, Root], the loop breaks at i=1 when the Root CA's pubkey hash matches ROOTCA_PUBKEY_HASH. The Root CA certificate (certs[2]) is never checked for expiry (notBefore/notAfter) or self-signature validity. An expired Root CA certificate would still anchor the trust chain.
threats.investigation_rationale: The loop at i=1 verifies certs[1] (Intermediate) against certs[2] (Root), then finds Root's pubkey matches ROOTCA_PUBKEY_HASH and breaks. It never reaches i=2 to check Root's own validity. While Intel's Root CA cert has a long validity period, if it expires, all attestations using it should fail but won't in this implementation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Loop breaks on ROOTCA_PUBKEY_HASH match without validating root cert's notBefore/notAfter or self-signature

preconditions: Intel Root CA certificate has expired (notAfter < block.timestamp)
Attestation quote submitted with expired root CA in cert chain

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: certChainCanBeTrusted = true when issuerPubKeyHash == ROOTCA_PUBKEY_HASH, without checking root cert expiry
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Expired root CA cert still anchors trust, accepting attestations that should be rejected
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Could enable SGX attestation with compromised-after-expiry root CA to register malicious SGX instances

economic_questions: When does Intel's Root CA certificate expire?
What security implications arise from accepting attestations after root CA expiry?
Could a compromised expired root CA key be used to forge attestations?
validation_requirements: Check Intel Root CA certificate validity period
Verify that no checks on certs[n-1] expiry occur elsewhere
Assess attack window if root CA key is compromised post-expiry

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Infinite if root CA key compromised

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After Intel Root CA certificate expires
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete compromise of SGX proof verification

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All protocol users relying on SGX proof verification
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete loss of proof integrity guarantees
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All bonded assets in the proving system

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Intel Root CA cert validity dates
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm loop never reaches root cert validation

threats.title: verifyParsedQuote External Function Bypasses Raw Quote Binary Validation
threats.hypothesis_description: The external verifyParsedQuote accepts a pre-parsed ParsedV3QuoteStruct and calls validateParsedInput which checks field lengths but NOT raw binary structure. The signedQuoteData used for signature verification is reconstructed via abi.encodePacked from struct fields. If the reconstruction differs from what was originally signed, or if struct fields pass length checks but contain semantically invalid data that parseInput would have caught, attestation integrity is compromised.
threats.investigation_rationale: parseInput validates raw binary: minimum quote length, localAuthDataSize consistency, header values. verifyParsedQuote skips these checks. An attacker could craft a ParsedV3QuoteStruct that passes validateParsedInput but represents data that would fail parseInput. The reconstructed signedQuoteData via abi.encodePacked may not match the Intel binary format exactly, potentially allowing signature verification to pass with non-standard data.

proof_of_signal.pattern: validateParsedInput checks struct field sizes but not raw binary consistency; signedQuoteData reconstructed via abi.encodePacked

preconditions: Attacker can call verifyParsedQuote directly (it's external)
Crafted ParsedV3QuoteStruct passes validateParsedInput length checks
Reconstructed signedQuoteData matches some valid ECDSA signature

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: signedQuoteData = abi.encodePacked(headerBytes, packQEReport(localEnclaveReport)) — may differ from original binary layout
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Bypassing parseInput's binary validation while passing validateParsedInput's struct validation
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Register fraudulent SGX instances using crafted attestation data

economic_questions: Does abi.encodePacked reconstruction exactly match Intel's binary format?
What parseInput validations are missing from validateParsedInput?
Can a valid ECDSA signature be found for reconstructed but non-standard data?
validation_requirements: Compare byte-by-byte output of parseInput vs validateParsedInput paths
Test if crafted struct can bypass header validation (version, teeType, vendorId)
Verify signature verification behavior with reconstructed signedQuoteData

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Protocol-level if SGX attestation is bypassed
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High if fraudulent SGX instances can be registered

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could undermine entire SGX proving tier

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol users relying on SGX proof integrity

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Diff parseInput vs validateParsedInput validation coverage
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Byte comparison of abi.encodePacked output vs raw binary format

threats.title: ERC20Airdrop2 Permanent Fund Lock if withdrawalWindow Initialized to Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: ERC20Airdrop2.init does not validate that _withdrawalWindow > 0. If initialized with _withdrawalWindow = 0, the getBalance function divides by withdrawalWindow, causing a division-by-zero revert. All calls to withdraw() would permanently revert, locking all claimed airdrop tokens forever.
threats.investigation_rationale: The deployment script doesn't show ERC20Airdrop2 deployment, so the initialization parameters are unknown. The contract's init function accepts _withdrawalWindow as uint64 with no zero check. getBalance computes: `balance * (...) / withdrawalWindow` which reverts if withdrawalWindow == 0.
deployment_assumptions: withdrawalWindow is set to 0 during initialization
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence for ERC20Airdrop2 initialization found in provided scripts; init function at ERC20Airdrop2.sol lines 56-71 has no validation for _withdrawalWindow > 0

proof_of_signal.pattern: No validation _withdrawalWindow > 0 in init; getBalance divides by withdrawalWindow

preconditions: Contract initialized with _withdrawalWindow = 0
Users have claimed tokens (claimedAmount > 0)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: timeBasedAllowance = balance * (...) / withdrawalWindow — division by zero when withdrawalWindow == 0
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Incorrect initialization permanently locks all claimed tokens
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All airdrop tokens become permanently unrecoverable

economic_questions: What is the planned withdrawalWindow value for deployment?
Can the owner recover from this by changing config?
Is there a rescue mechanism for locked funds?
validation_requirements: Verify init allows _withdrawalWindow = 0
Confirm getBalance reverts on division by zero
Check if setConfig can fix the issue (setConfig only changes claim params, not withdrawalWindow)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete loss of airdrop for all users

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm no setter exists for withdrawalWindow post-init

threats.title: TaikoL2 gasExcess Can Permanently Drop to Zero Disabling Dynamic Base Fee
threats.hypothesis_description: In _calc1559BaseFee, if the gas issuance (numL1Blocks * gasTargetPerL1Block) exceeds the current excess + parentGasUsed, gasExcess is set to 1. However, if gasExcess is ever set to 0 (e.g., via TaikoL2EIP1559Configurable.setConfigAndExcess), the entire basefee calculation is skipped and basefee returns 1. Since the code only adds parentGasUsed when gasExcess > 0, once at 0 it stays at 0 permanently — the dynamic EIP-1559 fee mechanism is irrecoverably disabled.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code has an explicit check: `if (gasExcess > 0)` before all basefee computation. When gasExcess == 0, basefee is always 1 wei. This enables extremely cheap L2 transactions indefinitely, potentially enabling spam attacks. While the minimum excess is clamped to 1 (not 0) in normal operation, the owner can set it to 0 via setConfigAndExcess.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (gasExcess > 0) gates all computation; gasExcess==0 means basefee permanently returns 1

preconditions: gasExcess reaches 0 (either via owner setConfigAndExcess or natural reduction)
Note: in normal operation, excess is clamped to min 1, so owner action is typically required

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: gasExcess == 0 → skip all computation → basefee = 1 forever
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Once gasExcess = 0, dynamic fees disabled permanently; no recovery without owner intervention
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Extremely cheap L2 transactions enable spam/griefing at near-zero cost

economic_questions: Can gasExcess naturally reach 0 without owner intervention?
What is the economic impact of basefee = 1 wei permanently?
Can setConfigAndExcess restore gasExcess > 0?
validation_requirements: Trace _calc1559BaseFee with gasExcess = 0
Verify excess.min(type(uint64).max) behavior at boundaries
Assess whether large issuance subtraction can underflow excess to 0

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Unlimited cheap L2 block space for spam

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: L2 becomes susceptible to state bloat and spam

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Current gasExcess value and typical excess dynamics
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify excess=1 minimum prevents reaching 0 in normal operation

threats.title: SigVerifyLib assert(success) Causes Full Gas Consumption on ES256VERIFIER Failure
threats.hypothesis_description: verifyES256Signature uses `assert(success)` after a staticcall to ES256VERIFIER. If ES256VERIFIER has no code deployed, is self-destructed, or returns unexpected data, the staticcall returns success=false. The assert then triggers a Panic(0x01) consuming ALL remaining gas. This makes every failed ES256 verification maximally expensive.
threats.investigation_rationale: assert() consumes all remaining gas on failure (Panic), unlike require() which refunds. If ES256VERIFIER becomes non-functional, every attestation verification that reaches ES256 signature checks will consume maximum gas and revert. This creates a gas griefing vector and effectively DoS's the attestation system.
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol deploys P256Verifier and passes its address to SigVerifyLib constructor; no proxy pattern for P256Verifier means it cannot be upgraded

proof_of_signal.pattern: assert(success) after ES256VERIFIER.staticcall — consumes all gas on failure instead of returning false

preconditions: ES256VERIFIER contract has no code (not deployed, self-destructed, or wrong address)
Attestation verification reaches ES256 signature check step

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: assert(success) on staticcall failure → Panic(0x01) → all gas consumed
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: If ES256VERIFIER is non-functional, every attestation call is maximally expensive
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Gas griefing: callers pay maximum gas for guaranteed revert

economic_questions: What is the maximum gas cost per failed ES256 verification?
Can ES256VERIFIER (P256Verifier) be self-destructed or become non-functional?
What is the cascading effect on SGX prover registration?
validation_requirements: Test verifyES256Signature with non-existent ES256VERIFIER
Measure gas consumption on assert failure
Verify P256Verifier cannot be self-destructed

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Gas waste per caller: up to block gas limit

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete DoS of SGX attestation with maximum gas cost

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Full gas consumption per failed call
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Gas costs across all attestation attempts

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check P256Verifier deployment and code presence
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Gas measurement with assert vs require on staticcall failure

threats.title: TaikoL2EIP1559Configurable Owner Can Manipulate Gas Pricing to Extreme Values
threats.hypothesis_description: setConfigAndExcess allows the owner to set gasExcess to type(uint64).max, causing the exponential basefee calculation to reach astronomical levels, making all L2 transactions prohibitively expensive. Alternatively, setting gasExcess to 0 permanently disables dynamic fees (basefee = 1 wei). The owner can also set basefeeAdjustmentQuotient to extreme values to distort the fee curve.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the function has basic validation (gasTargetPerL1Block != 0, basefeeAdjustmentQuotient != 0), there are no bounds on gasExcess. The owner (cross-chain via bridge) could set values that create extreme economic conditions for all L2 users. This is an admin abuse scenario but the impact is protocol-critical.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No bounds check on _newGasExcess; can be set to 0 (disable) or type(uint64).max (extreme basefee)

preconditions: Owner account (or cross-chain owner via bridge) is compromised
Owner calls setConfigAndExcess with extreme parameters

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: gasExcess = type(uint64).max → Lib1559Math.basefee returns maximum exponential value
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Owner sets gasExcess to 0 (disable fees) or max (prohibitive fees)
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Extreme basefee manipulation affects all L2 users immediately

economic_questions: What is the maximum basefee when gasExcess = type(uint64).max?
How quickly can the owner be stopped via governance?
What value bounds would be reasonable for gasExcess?
validation_requirements: Calculate basefee at gasExcess = type(uint64).max
Assess governance response time for emergency parameter changes
Model economic impact on L2 users under extreme fee scenarios

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect: economic disruption of entire L2

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Transactions become impossible (too expensive) or fee-free (spam risk)

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Cross-chain ownership security and governance response time
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Basefee calculation at extreme gasExcess values

threats.title: AutomataDcapV3Attestation Has No Ownership Transfer Mechanism
threats.hypothesis_description: AutomataDcapV3Attestation uses a custom owner pattern set in the constructor with no transfer or renounce mechanism. Unlike EssentialContract-based contracts which inherit Ownable2Step, this contract's owner is permanently fixed. If the owner key is lost or compromised, all admin functions (TCB configuration, certificate revocation, QE identity, trusted enclave management) become permanently inaccessible or permanently controlled by the attacker.
threats.investigation_rationale: The deployment script (DeployOnL1.s.sol) deploys AutomataDcapV3Attestation without a proxy (noted: 'No need to proxy these'). The owner is set to the deployer in the constructor. There is no transferOwnership function. If Intel updates TCB info, revokes certificates, or the security landscape changes, the owner must update the contract. Permanent loss of this capability is operationally catastrophic.
threats.config_evidence: DeployOnL1.s.sol deploys AutomataDcapV3Attestation directly (not proxied); owner is msg.sender; no ownership transfer in deployment script

proof_of_signal.pattern: owner = msg.sender in constructor; no transferOwnership, no renounceOwnership functions exist

preconditions: Owner private key is lost or compromised
Or owner needs to be transferred to a multisig/timelock for security

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: owner is write-once in constructor, no setter exists
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Compromised key gives permanent irrevocable control; lost key permanently freezes configuration
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Compromised owner can whitelist malicious enclaves, accept revoked certs, enable fraudulent attestations indefinitely

economic_questions: Is the current owner a multisig or EOA?
Can the contract be replaced via AddressManager registration?
What is the value at risk from permanent malicious control of attestation config?
validation_requirements: Check if rollupAddressManager can swap 'automata_dcap_attestation' address
Assess if swapping the registered address is sufficient mitigation
Evaluate operational risk of permanent owner lock

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High if SGX proving is economically significant

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Undermines entire SGX proof verification tier

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All protocol participants relying on SGX proofs

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Current owner address and key management
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify no hidden ownership transfer mechanism
